hero.tagline: Illinois small claims courts handle disputes up to $10,000 with streamlined procedures and optional attorney representation.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Filing fee
at_a_glance.detail: Varies by county and claim amount

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Appeal must be filed within 30 days of judgment

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: As of October 1, 2025, appearance fees in small claims cases have been eliminated

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $10,000, you cannot split it into multiple small claims cases. You must either waive the amount over $10,000 to file in small claims court or file your case in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and Money Owed
claim_categories.blurb: Illinois allows you 10 years to file a lawsuit for breach of a written contract and 5 years for an oral contract. Ensure you file within these timeframes to preserve your claim.

claims.name: Breach of a Written Contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: You have 10 years from the date of breach to file your claim. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an Oral Contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $2,000, and they failed to repay as promised.
claims.notes: You have 5 years from the date of breach to file your claim. Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails, texts, or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid Personal Loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $3,000 with the understanding they would repay in six months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: If the loan was documented in writing, you have 10 years to file; if it was an oral agreement, you have 5 years. Provide any documentation or communications regarding the loan.

claims.name: Default on a Promissory Note
claims.example: Someone signed a promissory note to repay $5,000 but has defaulted on payments.
claims.notes: You have 10 years from the date of default to file your claim. Present the original promissory note and any records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid Invoice for Services Rendered
claims.example: You provided graphic design services worth $1,500, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: For written agreements, you have 10 years to file; for oral agreements, 5 years. Include the invoice, any contracts, and communication records.

claims.name: Unpaid Credit Card Debt
claims.example: A customer owes $8,000 on a credit card and has stopped making payments.
claims.notes: Credit card debts are typically considered written contracts, giving you 10 years to file. Provide account statements and records of communication.

claims.name: Unpaid Rent by Tenant
claims.example: A tenant moved out owing $2,500 in unpaid rent.
claims.notes: You have 10 years to file for unpaid rent under a written lease. Bring the lease agreement, payment records, and any notices sent to the tenant.

claims.name: Recovery for a Bad Check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $500 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: You can recover the amount of the check plus damages. Provide the original check, bank notices, and any related correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $1,500 or triple the check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer Seeking Repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $7,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible.
claims.notes: As a co-signer, you have 10 years to seek repayment from the primary borrower. Present the loan agreement and records of payments you've made.

claim_categories.title: Consumer Disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Illinois provides strong consumer protections, including the Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act, which allows for recovery of damages and attorney's fees.

claims.name: Purchase of a Defective Product
claims.example: You bought a $900 appliance that stopped working after two weeks, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file under the Consumer Fraud Act. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty information, and any communication with the seller.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney's fees and punitive damages if the violation was willful or reckless.

claims.name: Substandard Service
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $600 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: File within 3 years under the Consumer Fraud Act. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and evidence of the substandard service.
claims.damage_boost: Potential recovery of attorney's fees and punitive damages for willful or reckless violations.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory Auto Repair
claims.example: You paid $1,200 for car repairs, but the problem persists, and the mechanic refuses to fix it.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file under the Consumer Fraud Act. Bring repair invoices, payment records, and any communication with the mechanic.
claims.damage_boost: Attorney's fees and punitive damages may be awarded for willful or reckless violations.

claims.name: Home Contractor Fraud
claims.example: A contractor took a $5,000 deposit for home improvements but never started the work.
claims.notes: File within 3 years under the Consumer Fraud Act. Provide the contract, payment proof, and any correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney's fees and punitive damages for willful or reckless conduct.

claims.name: Vendor Fails to Deliver Services
claims.example: You paid $2,000 for a wedding photographer who never showed up and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file under the Consumer Fraud Act. Bring the service contract, payment records, and any communication.
claims.damage_boost: Attorney's fees and punitive damages may be available for willful or reckless violations.

claims.name: Unauthorized Subscription Charges
claims.example: A company continues to charge your credit card $50 monthly despite multiple cancellation requests.
claims.notes: File within 3 years under the Consumer Fraud Act. Provide records of cancellation requests and billing statements.
claims.damage_boost: Potential recovery of attorney's fees and punitive damages for willful or reckless violations.

claims.name: Purchase of a Defective Used Car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $9,000 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: You have 3 years to file under the Consumer Fraud Act. Bring the sales contract, payment proof, and any communication with the dealer.
claims.damage_boost: Attorney's fees and punitive damages may be awarded for willful or reckless violations.

claims.name: False Advertising Claims
claims.example: A store advertised a product for $100, but charged you $150 at checkout without honoring the advertised price.
claims.notes: File within 3 years under the Consumer Fraud Act. Provide the advertisement, receipt, and any communication with the store.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney's fees and punitive damages for willful or reckless conduct.

claim_categories.title: Property Damage
claim_categories.blurb: Claims for property damage in Illinois must be filed within 5 years. Ensure you document all damages and related expenses thoroughly.

claims.name: Damage from a Vehicle Collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $4,000 in damage to your car and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: You have 5 years from the date of the accident to file. Provide the police report, repair estimates, and any communication with the other driver.

claims.name: Neighbor's Actions Damaging Your Property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell onto your property, causing $3,500 in damage to your fence.
claims.notes: File within 5 years of the incident. Bring photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with your neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant Causing Damage to Rental Property
claims.example: A tenant caused $2,000 in damages beyond normal wear and tear and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: You have 5 years to file. Provide the lease agreement, photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the tenant.

claims.name: Damage Caused by Someone Else's Pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,500 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: File within 5 years of the incident. Bring photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to Property Left in Someone's Care
claims.example: You left a $2,000 watch with a jeweler for repair, and they damaged it.
claims.notes: You have 5 years to file. Provide the repair agreement, photos of the damage, and any communication with the jeweler.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-Tenant Disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Illinois law provides specific protections for tenants, including timely return of security deposits and habitable living conditions.

claims.name: Failure to Return Security Deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: Landlords must return the deposit or provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days. File within 5 years if they fail to do so.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of up to two times the deposit amount if the landlord acted in bad faith.

claims.name: No Itemized List for Withheld Deposit
claims.example: Your landlord kept $800 of your deposit without explaining why.
claims.notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days. File within 5 years if they fail to do so.
claims.damage_boost: Potential recovery of up to two times the deposit amount for bad faith withholding.

claims.name: Uninhabitable Living Conditions
claims.example: Your apartment had no heat during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it.
claims.notes: Tenants can withhold rent or terminate the lease if the landlord fails to maintain habitable conditions. File within 5 years.

claims.name: Landlord Keeping Tenant's Property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your $2,000 worth of furniture.
claims.notes: Landlords must follow legal procedures when handling a tenant's abandoned property. File within 5 years if they fail to do so.

claims.name: Unlawful Eviction Practices
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: Self-help evictions are illegal in Illinois. Tenants can sue for damages and reinstatement. File within 5 years.

claims.name: Failure to Return Prepaid Rent
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but the landlord refuses to return the last month's rent after you moved out.
claims.notes: Landlords must return prepaid rent if not applied. File within 5 years if they fail to do so.

claims.name: Tenant Owes Unpaid Rent
claims.example: A tenant moved out owing $3,000 in unpaid rent.
claims.notes: Landlords have 10 years to file for unpaid rent under a written lease. Provide the lease agreement and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant Repair and Deduct
claims.example: You paid $500 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord failed to do so and deducted it from your rent.
claims.notes: Tenants can repair and deduct if the landlord fails to address issues affecting habitability. File within 5 years if the landlord disputes.

claim_categories.title: Employment and Wages
claim_categories.blurb: Illinois law mandates timely payment of wages and provides penalties for employers who fail to comply.

claims.name: Unpaid Wages
claims.example: Your employer failed to pay you $1,000 for hours worked.
claims.notes: Employees have 10 years to file for unpaid wages. Provide pay stubs, time records, and any communication with the employer.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of 2% of the underpayment per month as a penalty.

claims.name: Failure to Provide Final Paycheck
claims.example: After termination, your employer did not give you your final paycheck of $800.
claims.notes: Employers must provide the final paycheck by the next scheduled payday. File within 10 years if they fail to do so.
claims.damage_boost: Potential recovery of 2% of the underpayment per month as a penalty.

claims.name: Unpaid Sales Commissions
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,500 in earned commissions but refuses to pay.
claims.notes: Commissions are considered wages. File within 10 years and provide the commission agreement and sales records.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of 2% of the underpayment per month as a penalty.

claims.name: Unpaid Overtime Wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but were paid only your regular rate.
claims.notes: Employers must pay overtime at 1.5 times the regular rate. File within 10 years and provide time records and pay stubs.
claims.damage_boost: Potential recovery of 2% of the underpayment per month as a penalty.

claim_categories.title: Personal Claims
claim_categories.blurb: Personal injury claims in Illinois must be filed within 2 years, while property damage claims have a 5-year statute of limitations.

claims.name: Minor Personal Injury
claims.example: You slipped and fell in a store, resulting in $3,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: You have 2 years from the date of injury to file. Provide medical records, bills, and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to Return Loaned Property
claims.example: You loaned a friend a $1,500 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: File within 5 years of the demand for return. Provide evidence of ownership and any communication regarding the loan.

claims.name: Theft of Personal Property
claims.example: Someone stole your $2,000 bicycle, and you have evidence of their involvement.
claims.notes: You have 5 years to file. Provide police reports, evidence of ownership, and any proof of the defendant's involvement.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-Specific Claims
claim_categories.blurb: Illinois allows claims related to vehicle repairs and wrongful towing, with specific statutes governing these disputes.

claims.name: Dispute Over Mechanic's Charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $1,200 for repairs you didn't authorize.
claims.notes: File within 3 years under the Consumer Fraud Act. Provide repair invoices, payment records, and any communication with the mechanic.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney's fees and punitive damages for willful or reckless violations.

claims.name: Wrongful Towing of Vehicle
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and the towing company demands $500 for its return.
claims.notes: Towing companies must follow specific procedures. File within 5 years if they fail to do so.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are handled in the Circuit Court but may not be suitable for small claims procedures due to their complexity.
Disputes involving ownership or title to real property are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims involving defamation, libel, or slander are not suitable for small claims court due to the complexity of proving damages.
Professional malpractice claims require expert testimony and are beyond the scope of small claims court.
Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are handled by family courts, not small claims court.
Disputes involving wills, estates, and trusts are under the jurisdiction of probate courts.
Small claims courts do not have jurisdiction over cases against federal agencies or the U.S. government.
Class action lawsuits are complex and not suitable for the simplified procedures of small claims court.
Small claims courts cannot issue orders requiring a party to do or refrain from doing something.
These claims are handled by the Illinois Workers' Compensation Commission, not small claims court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are generally not awarded in small claims court. However, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as consumer fraud violations.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $25 + civil penalty up to $1,500 or triple the check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney's fees may be recoverable in cases involving violations of the Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act or other statutes that specifically allow for fee recovery.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the Circuit Court

filing_options: County where the transaction or injury occurred
County where the corporation does business
County where its principal office is located

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in the case being transferred or dismissed, leading to delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability varies by county. Check with the local court clerk for specific procedures.

service_fees.notes: Costs vary; ensure the server is licensed.

forms.number: Application for Waiver of Court Fees
forms.name: Application for Waiver of Court Fees

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% of the federal poverty line
Other circumstances showing inability to pay fees
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees, service fees, and other court costs.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are typically not added to the judgment against the loser.

other_fees.name: Jury demand fee
other_fees.notes: Additional fee if a jury trial is requested.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are typically added to the judgment against the loser.

entries.notes: Ensure you file within 10 years to preserve your claim.
File within 5 years to maintain your right to sue.
Applicable to contracts for the sale of goods.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge
entries.notes: File within 5 years to preserve your claim.
Discovery rule applies; file within 5 years of discovery.
File within 3 years to recover damages.
Employees have 10 years to file for unpaid wages.

entries.clock_starts: Date deposit was due to be returned
entries.notes: File within 5 years if the deposit is not returned.
Judgments are enforceable for 7 years; can be revived.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, allowing the statute of limitations to begin when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: Statutes of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or other specific circumstances apply.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad checks, a written demand for payment must be sent, and if unpaid after 30 days, you may recover damages up to three times the check amount.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: Employees must make a written demand for unpaid wages; if unpaid, they may recover penalties of 2% per month on the underpayment.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days of lease termination; failure to do so may result in penalties.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: Under the Consumer Fraud Act, consumers can recover attorney's fees and punitive damages for willful or reckless violations.

forms.name: Complaint
forms.description: Initiates the small claims lawsuit by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and desired relief.

forms.name: Summons
forms.description: Notifies the defendant of the lawsuit and the date of the court hearing.

forms.name: Affidavit of Service
forms.description: Proof that the defendant was properly served with the summons and complaint.

forms.name: Appearance
forms.description: Filed by the defendant to indicate their intention to participate in the case.

forms.name: Answer
forms.description: The defendant's formal response to the allegations in the complaint.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Filed by the defendant if they have a claim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Motion to Vacate Default Judgment
forms.description: Request to set aside a default judgment entered against a party who failed to appear.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed by the plaintiff to indicate that the judgment has been paid in full.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Filed to initiate an appeal of the small claims court's decision.

forms.name: Application for Waiver of Court Fees
forms.description: Request to waive court fees due to financial hardship.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: A sheriff or deputy personally delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.
cons: May be slower than private service

methods.name: Certified mail
methods.description: The court clerk sends the summons and complaint to the defendant via certified mail with return receipt requested.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed private individual delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent of the corporation or, if none, an officer or agent of the corporation. For sole proprietors, serve the individual owner.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Illinois law and the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located after diligent efforts, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document all attempts and seek court approval for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear in court on the date specified in the summons.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim before a judgment is entered.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal the decision within 30 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to the regular civil docket.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys, but it is not required.
hearing.format: Bench trial with each side presenting evidence and witnesses; typically lasts 30-60 minutes.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter from the court clerk as soon as possible before the hearing date.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 7 days
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services on the day of the hearing; check with the local court.

appeals.type_notes: The appellate court reviews the record of the small claims court proceedings; no new evidence is presented.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed, but it is often more effective to file a motion to vacate the judgment first.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues at 9% per annum on judgments.

methods.name: Wage Garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a wage deduction order with the court, serve the employer, and the employer will withhold a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: The lesser of 15% of gross weekly wages or the amount by which disposable earnings exceed 45 times the federal minimum wage per week.

methods.name: Bank Levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a non-wage garnishment order from the court, serve the bank, and the bank will freeze and turn over funds from the debtor's account.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account; timing is crucial to prevent withdrawals.
exemptions: $4,000 in personal property, including bank accounts, is exempt from collection.

methods.name: Property Lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property, preventing its sale or refinancing until the judgment is satisfied.
methods.description: Record the judgment with the county recorder's office to create a lien on the debtor's real estate.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real estate; may take time to realize payment, as the lien is satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
exemptions: Homestead exemption up to $15,000 for individuals, $30,000 for married couples.

methods.name: Writ of Execution
methods.blurb: Authorize the sheriff to seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, and the sheriff will seize and auction the debtor's non-exempt assets.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt property; process can be time-consuming and may not yield full payment.
exemptions: $4,000 in personal property is exempt; specific items like necessary clothing and tools of the trade are also exempt.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a petition to revive the judgment before the 7-year period expires to extend its enforceability.
exempt_assets: Homestead — $15,000 for individuals, $30,000 for married couples
Social Security and federal benefits — fully exempt
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged, while others, like those for fraud, may survive.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized based on the date of recording; earlier liens have higher priority over later ones.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Corporations and LLCs may represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under an assumed name must file a certificate with the county clerk.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may authorize an employee to represent them in small claims court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts; ensure proper licensing before filing a claim.

county_variations.detail: Cook County requires e-filing for all small claims cases through the Odyssey eFileIL system.
DuPage County offers mandatory mediation for small claims disputes before proceeding to trial.
Lake County's filing fees for small claims cases range from $50 to $150, depending on the claim amount.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: Suing the wrong party or using incorrect names can result in dismissal or unenforceable judgments.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing; for businesses, check the Secretary of State's records.

pitfalls.title: Missing statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations expires leads to automatic dismissal of the case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Confirm the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within the allowed time frame.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to serve the defendant correctly can delay the case or result in dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Illinois rules for service of process carefully, using authorized methods and ensuring timely service.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of adequate evidence can lead to losing the case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather and organize all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements before the hearing.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring court deadlines
pitfalls.what_happens: Missing filing deadlines or court dates can result in default judgments or case dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Keep track of all deadlines and court dates, and ensure timely submissions and appearances.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: Failing to file a counterclaim may forfeit your right to seek damages related to the same dispute.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: If you have a claim against the plaintiff, file a counterclaim within the allowed time frame.

recent_changes.title: Elimination of appearance fees in small claims cases
recent_changes.description: The Illinois Supreme Court amended rules to eliminate appearance fees in small claims cases, reducing financial barriers for defendants.

recent_changes.title: Fee waivers expanded for minor guardianship cases
recent_changes.description: Filing and appearance fees were eliminated in minor guardianship cases to improve access to justice.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Illinois small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves. However, you may choose to hire an attorney if you prefer.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The timeline varies by county, but most small claims cases are resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing to judgment.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: In Illinois small claims court, you can sue for up to $10,000, excluding interest and court costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear in court, the judge may enter a default judgment in your favor. You may still need to provide evidence to support your claim before the judgment is finalized.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims court decision. The appeal must be filed within 30 days of the judgment, and the appellate court will review the record of the original proceedings.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: In Illinois, a judgment is enforceable for 7 years. You can petition the court to revive the judgment before it expires to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Illinois allows corporations and LLCs to represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney.
The Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act provides for recovery of attorney's fees and punitive damages in cases of willful or reckless violations.
Landlords must return security deposits or provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days of lease termination.
Wage garnishment in Illinois is limited to the lesser of 15% of gross weekly wages or the amount by which disposable earnings exceed 45 times the federal minimum wage per week.
Judgments accrue interest at a rate of 9% per annum until satisfied.

interesting_findings: Illinois has eliminated appearance fees in small claims cases as of October 1, 2025, reducing financial barriers for defendants.
The state provides a comprehensive set of standardized forms for small claims proceedings, available on the Illinois Courts website.
Some counties in Illinois offer mandatory mediation services for small claims disputes to encourage settlement before trial.
E-filing requirements and availability vary by county; litigants should check with their local court for specific procedures.
Illinois law provides specific exemptions for wage garnishment and property liens to protect debtors from undue hardship.

sources.label: Illinois Supreme Court Rule 282 (Small Claims Procedures)
Illinois Courts Approved Statewide Forms - Small Claims
Illinois Courts Fee Schedule by County

Illinois Supreme Court Rule 301 (Appeals from Final Judgments)
815 ILCS 505/ Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act
820 ILCS 115/1 et seq. (Illinois Wage Payment and Collection Act)
